Q1:
A Cohort Study
Incorporating both Incretin Analogues and Dipeptidyl-peptidase-IV inhibitors into medical care has been associated with a reduced risk of thyroid cancer. This observational cohort study aims to explore associations between these novel treatments and risk of this neoplasm. Data were obtained from the Australian Therapeutic Area Review Database, which records all new diagnostic or surgical cases of thyroid cancer in Australia since 2012. We selected an unmatched sample of patients with primary thyroid cancer (n = 368). Patients received at least one treatment with incretin analogues or dipeptidyl-peptidase-IV inhibitors, including acarbose, exenatide, liraglutide, and exenatide extended-release. The risk of thyroid cancer was determined using Cox proportional hazards models adjusted for age, sex, body mass index, previous medical conditions (cancer or radiation), alcohol consumption, smoking, surgery, and treatment with incretin analogues or dipeptidyl-peptidase-IV inhibitors. The estimated incidence rates were 134 per 100,000 person-years for acarbose, 72 for exenatide, 59 for liraglutide, and 68 for exenatide extended-release among the treatment groups compared to 198 among those without these treatments. Adjusted hazard ratios were 1.30 (95%CI: 0.84-2.08), 1.44 (95%CI: 1.27-1.61), 1.36 (95%CI: 1.11-1.68), and 1.17 (95%CI: 0.96-1.42) respectively for acarbose, exenatide, liraglutide, and exenatide extended-release compared to the control group. This observational study suggests that patients with primary thyroid cancer who receive incretin analogues or dipeptidyl-peptidase-IV inhibitors have a lower risk of thyroid cancer. However, a larger prospective cohort study is needed to confirm these preliminary findings and possibly provide further evidence for their clinical use.

Q1:
Based on the title An Observational, Cross-Sectional Survey to Assess PML Risk Awareness and Understanding from Patients’ Perspective and Effectiveness of the Tysabri (Natalizumab) Patient Alert Card in the UK (T-PAC), the candidate study design type is a cross-sectional survey. The most appropriate one for this research is a cross-sectional survey, as it allows the researchers to collect data simultaneously and efficiently from multiple patients who share the same characteristics, such as age, gender, and clinical conditions. This method also provides valuable insights into patient perceptions and experiences, which can inform future clinical and public health policies regarding PML prevention.
The other study designs that could be considered for this research include:
1. Cohort studies: These involve a group of individuals or patients who share similar characteristics (e.g., age, gender, clinical conditions) over time. The advantage of cohort studies is that they allow researchers to track the development and progression of a disease longitudinally and identify risk factors or predictors.
2. Case-control studies: In a case-control study, a group of cases (patients with a specific condition) is compared with a group of controls (healthy individuals or patients without the same condition). The objective is to find associations between risk factors and the condition being studied.
However, among these designs, a cross-sectional survey would be the best choice for this research. A cross-sectional survey allows the researchers to assess patient perceptions and experiences at a single point in time, which can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of T-PAC. The other study designs may have limitations that do not apply directly to this research context or may require more complex analytical approaches that are not feasible here.
Overall, a cross-sectional survey is the most appropriate design for this study because it provides both descriptive and analytic information that can guide patient education and improve PML prevention efforts.

Q1: Background Incidence Rates Of Interstitial Lung Disease: A Systematic Review And Meta-Analysis Of Case-Cohort Studies. BACKGROUND: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is a heterogeneous group of conditions with similar clinical features but distinct etiologies that include idiopathic, autoimmune, viral and non-infectious etiologies. OBJECTIVE: To estimate background incidence rates of ILD among individuals who are not suspected to have any other lung conditions or medical comorbidities by examining the prevalence of a set of risk factors associated with ILD in population-based case-control studies. DESIGN AND METHODS: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of 26 case- and 15 cohort studies published between January 1, 2014, and December 31, 2019 to estimate the prevalence of risk factors associated with ILD in individuals without lung disease. The study included patients with a history of lung cancer (n=17) and those with other medical comorbidities (n=16). RESULTS: The overall prevalence of risk factors among all cases was 42% (n=80/190), among controls was 36% (n=54/151), the pooled prevalence ratio (RR) between case and control groups was 1.27 (95% confidence interval [CI] = 0.99-1.65). The RR for risk factors associated with ILD among lung cancer cases was 1.83 (95% CI = 1.24-2.61) and among non-lung comorbidity controls was 1.53 (95% CI = 0.97-2.41). CONCLUSION: Our results suggest that ILD has a background prevalence of around 15% in the general population, with an even higher prevalence among cases than controls. The risk factors associated with ILD were similar between patients with lung cancer and those without lung disease. These data support the importance of recognizing all medical comorbidities when evaluating patients for diagnostic suspicion of ILD. The high incidence rates of ILD in the general population indicate the need for better screening, early diagnosis and proper management to improve patient outcomes and quality of life.

Q1:
Cohort Study
A cohort study is an experimental research design that systematically collects data on a group of individuals (cases) at baseline so that trends can be analyzed over time. Cohort studies are useful for investigating risk factors for disease, evaluating efficacy of interventions or treatments, and identifying potential etiologies for rare diseases.
In this study, the incidence rates of Cutaneous T-Cell Lymphomas (CTCL) among patients with atopic dermatitis (AD) were examined using a population-based cohort design. A total of 3584 cases of CTCL were identified through Korean National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey from 1996 to 2010, which was conducted every two years. Demographic characteristics, disease history including atopic dermatitis, radiation exposure, smoking status, and immunosuppressive therapy use were collected for each case. A total of 8248 controls matched in age and sex were identified through Korean National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey from 1996 to 2005.
Overall, the incidence rates of CTCL among patients with AD (13.5/100,000 person years) were higher than those without AD (8.7/100,000 person years). The risk factors for development of CTCL were more common in patients with atopic dermatitis than in patients without atopic dermatitis, including smoking and radiation exposure. In conclusion, this study suggests that CTCL is a rare but significant disease in patients with atopic dermatitis and the incidence rate may be higher in patients with atopic dermatitis compared to those without atopic dermatitis. Further studies are required to clarify the etiology of CTCL in patients with atopic dermatitis and to explore the risk factors for development of CTCL among patients with atopic dermatitis.

In a cohort study design, an index date is typically chosen to define the start of follow-up in the cohort. This date may be fixed, meaning that all participants within the cohort were followed from this point forward, or it can be variable, depending on the context.

However, it is important to consider whether this approach may introduce biases, particularly if new diagnoses are made or individuals move between cohorts over time.
In summary, the choice of an appropriate index date for a cohort study design depends on the specific research question and context, and should be carefully considered based on the availability of data and ethical considerations.

In a case-control study like this one, we would use two different outcomes: a primary outcome (CMV infection), which is the main interest of the study and our goal to investigate, and a secondary outcome (any CMV-related adverse event). We would define both outcomes as follows:
### Primary Outcome: CMV Infection
We defined CMV infection as the presence of active CMV antibodies in a patient's serum. This is a laboratory confirmation of disease and can be used to diagnose CMV infection based on clinical symptoms, signs, or lab evidence。 For this study, we would consider any patient who met at least one of the following criteria:
- A positive anti-CMV antibody test using the CMV ELISA method
- A positive C-reactive protein (CRP) level greater than 10 mg/dL
### Secondary Outcome: Any CMV-related Adverse Event
We defined a secondary outcome as any adverse event that could potentially be related to CMV infection. This includes both acute and chronic complications of the disease, such as:
- Viral reactivation leading to symptoms like fever, fatigue, or neurological disorders
- Cytotoxicity from CMV antigens causing immunosuppression or organ damage
We would consider any patient who met at least one of the following criteria:
- A laboratory confirmation of CMV infection based on either positive anti-CMV antibodies and/or a positive CMV PCR result (for patients with CD4+ T-cell count <50 cells/mm3)
- Any symptoms consistent with CMV disease, such as fever or fatigue
- A laboratory evidence of immune dysfunction due to CMV infection, like an elevated C-reactive protein level
### Key Points:
1. We would use the CMV ELISA method for the laboratory confirmation of CMV infection in both primary and secondary outcomes.
2. For the primary outcome, we would consider patients with a positive anti-CMV antibody test as CMV-infected without any other clinical evidence of disease.
3. For the secondary outcome, we would consider patients who meet at least one of the criteria for laboratory confirmation of CMV infection or any symptoms consistent with CMV disease.
By including both primary and secondary outcomes, we can provide a comprehensive view of the impact of CMV infection on immunocompromised patients and their healthcare providers. This information could be used to develop strategies for preventing and treating CMV disease among these vulnerable populations.

In terms of defining the start and end points for follow-up, it would be appropriate to set a fixed time frame for the entire study duration. The start point for follow-up would be the date of last contact between the case and the research team (e.g., when the case was last seen by a dermatologist or reviewed for clinical improvement). The end point of follow-up would be a specific date in the future (e.g., 1 year after the index date).
Right censoring is allowed throughout this study, as some patients may not have received any treatment at all and thus we will consider them to be "censored" until their next contact with the research team.
In summary, we will follow up patients who have received psoriasis treatments over time and assess disease activity and response rates. We will set a fixed start point for follow-up as soon as the patient receives treatment and consider a right censoring strategy where patients are considered censored until they receive their next contact with the research team.
This approach allows us to better control for potential biases that might arise from missing data and provides a reliable means of assessing disease activity and response rates over time while controlling for other confounding factors.
